_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_

This project is a **frontend-only React SPA** (single-page app), likely a class/lab project for a small “quirky fruity drinks” storefront concept.

## What it is

- Built with **Vite + React 19** (`vite`, `@vitejs/plugin-react`, `react`, `react-dom`)
- Uses **React Router** for page navigation (`/` and `/About`)
- Uses **MUI** (`@mui/material`, icons, emotion) for UI pieces like buttons, badge, snackbar
- Main features:
  - Home page with hero + product cards
  - About page
  - Navbar with mobile hamburger
  - Cart badge count and “added to cart” snackbar

## Current architecture

- Entry: `src/main.jsx` mounts `App`
- Routing/state: `src/App.jsx`
- Pages: `src/pages/Home.jsx`, `src/pages/About.jsx`
- Reusable components: `src/components/Navbar.jsx`, `src/components/ProductCard.jsx`
- Styling: mostly custom CSS in `src/Home.css` and `src/components/Navbar.css`, plus default Vite CSS in `src/index.css`

## What this suggests about the project scope

- It looks like an **early-stage UI prototype / learning app**, not production-ready ecommerce
- Product list is **hardcoded** in the Home page
- No backend/API/auth/database integration detected
- No tests/config for CI found in the inspected files

## Notable risks/issues I spotted

- `Home.jsx` imports several images (`src/assets/img/...`) but `src/assets` currently only contains `react.svg`  
  → likely build/runtime import errors unless those files exist elsewhere and weren’t included.
- `App.jsx` has suspicious cart logic:
  - checks `if (cartCount < 0)` right after incrementing
  - attempts to assign to `cartCount` (state variable), which is invalid React pattern
- CSS from default Vite template (`src/index.css`) may conflict with your custom page styling (global `:root`, `body`, `button` rules).

If you want, I can do a second pass and give you a **“production readiness checklist”** plus a prioritized fix plan (quick wins first).

Yes — I **do need the source images** to make it match the template closely.

I can read the long preview image you sent, but I can’t directly extract assets/layers from the `.psd` in this environment.  
So the best path is:

- Put all template images (characters, logos, backgrounds, juice product images, icons) into `my-spa-project/src/assets/template/`
- Keep clear names (example: `hero-left.png`, `hero-right.png`, `pineapple.png`, `footer-logo.png`)
- If possible, also add fonts used in template (or tell me their names)

Once you share them, I’ll implement the site to mirror the template section-by-section:
- top hero + promo strip
- favorites/menu blocks
- product cards row
- mid hero + character strip
- testimonial + newsletter
- footer

If you want, I can also start **immediately with layout + exact spacing/colors/typography from the preview**, then plug in your real images as soon as you upload them.

### What I changed

- Reworked `Home` into a full template-style page in `src/pages/Home.jsx`:
  - hero area with characters + headline + CTA
  - black promo strip with burst text
  - “All time favorites” feature block
  - summer promo section
  - 3-product row/cards
  - secondary hero section
  - orange refresh banner + character lineup
  - testimonial + stars
  - newsletter form
  - dark footer with logo, links, and character

- Replaced and cleaned styling in `src/Home.css`:
  - applied cream grid background, playful typography feel, pill buttons, section spacing
  - responsive behavior for mobile/tablet
  - decorative burst assets integrated

- Updated navbar to match the template direction in:
  - `src/components/Navbar.jsx`
  - `src/components/Navbar.css`
  - now uses `Title.png`, anchor links (`home/menu/gallery/contact`), sticky rounded nav, cart badge styling.

- Fixed cart logic bug in `src/App.jsx`:
  - corrected state update (`setCartCount(prev => prev + 1)`)
  - removed invalid state mutation
  - kept snackbar success message and positioned it top-center

- Removed default Vite style conflicts in `src/index.css` (minimal reset only).

### Verification

- Lint check: clean
- Build check: successful (`npm run build` passed)

  margin: 0;
  font-family: 'Fredoka', 'Baloo 2', sans-serif;
  font-size: 1.85rem;
}

.feature-content p {
  margin: 8px 0 0;
  max-width: 620px;
  font-size: 1rem;
  line-height: 1.45;
}

.feature-actions {
  margin-top: 12px;
  display: flex;
  gap: 10px;
}

.energy-section {
  margin-top: 14px;
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr auto;
  align-items: center;
  gap: 12px;
}

.energy-content h2 {
  margin: 0;
  font-family: 'Fredoka', 'Baloo 2', sans-serif;
  font-size: clamp(2.2rem, 4.8vw, 4.8rem);
  line-height: 0.9;
  text-transform: uppercase;
}

.energy-visual-wrap {
  position: relative;
  width: min(380px, 42vw);
}

.burst-solid {
  width: 100%;
}

.energy-visual {
  position: absolute;
  right: 8%;
  top: 9%;
  width: 74%;
}

the below css is applied and shown behind testimonial class. can you tell me how can I modify it. Like i want to take this background a little upwards
.quirky-page::before {
  background: url('./assets/img/Ellipse Solid.png') center top / 320px auto repeat;
}

.quirky-page::after {
  background: url('./assets/img/Ellipse Outline.png') left top / 340px auto repeat;
  opacity: 0.18;
}

---

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Explaining background-position**

I need to answer a question without any tools, so I’ll explain the pseudo-element `background-position`. It can indeed use values like `center -30px`. I might also mention changing `inset` or `top` and using `transform translateY` with a negative value. Additionally, there’s the shorthand for `background-position` that can simplify how it’s applied. I’ll keep my explanation concise to make it as clear as possible for the user!</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_

Yes — easiest way is to change the **background position Y** value.

Right now both are at `top` (same as `0px`).  
To move them **upwards**, give a negative Y offset.

```css
.quirky-page::before {
  background: url('./assets/img/Ellipse Solid.png') center -30px / 320px auto repeat;
}

.quirky-page::after {
  background: url('./assets/img/Ellipse Outline.png') left -30px / 340px auto repeat;
  opacity: 0.18;
}
```

### How to tune

- `-10px` = slightly up
- `-30px` = medium up
- `-60px` = more up

---

Alternative (same effect, sometimes clearer):

```css
.quirky-page::before {
  background: url('./assets/img/Ellipse Solid.png') center top / 320px auto repeat;
  transform: translateY(-30px);
}

.quirky-page::after {
  background: url('./assets/img/Ellipse Outline.png') left top / 340px auto repeat;
  opacity: 0.18;
  transform: translateY(-30px);
}
```

If you want, I can apply the exact value directly in your file (`-20px`, `-30px`, etc.).

Here’s what was implemented to match your footer screenshot.

### Layout & style
- **Full-width** black footer (`#000000`), no max-width constraint on the footer itself (removed `.footer` from the shared content width rule).
- **Logo** top-left (`Title.png`), larger.
- **Four columns** of links with the same labels as your reference:
  - Home, About, Menu  
  - Smoothies, Juices, Fruits  
  - Imports, Branches, Social Media  
  - History, Job Vacancies  
- **Coral links** (`#e85d44`), **bold**, **underlined**.
- **Orange character** (`22.png` / `CharacterThree`) **bottom-right**, partly clipped (`overflow: hidden` on footer + absolute positioning).

### Files touched
- `src/pages/Home.jsx` — new footer markup; removed unused `FooterFace` import.
- `src/Home.css` — footer styles + responsive: under `920px`, columns become 2×2 and the mascot stacks below.

Build and lints are clean. If you want the mascot **larger** or **more** cut off at the edge, say how much and we can nudge `right` / `bottom` / `width`.

---

Here’s what was added:

### `Navbar.jsx`
- Imported MUI `Badge` and `ShoppingCartIcon`.
- Navbar accepts **`count`** (default `0`).
- Cart + badge sit **after** the nav links and **before** the hamburger.

### `App.jsx`
- Passes **`count={cartCount}`** so the badge matches add-to-cart actions.

### `Navbar.css`
- `.nav-cart-wrap` for alignment and spacing.
- On small screens, cart uses **`margin-left: auto`** so it stays on the right with the menu button.

The badge uses **`color="warning"`** (amber) like before; it shows **0** when the cart is empty (`showZero`).

If you want the icon to scroll to `#menu` or open a cart drawer, say which behavior you prefer.

**Updating cart logic**

I'm adding +/- buttons beside the Add to Cart button to adjust quantity. I'm implementing compact quantity controls with - and + buttons adjacent to the Add to Cart button. I'm implementing compact quantity controls with - and + buttons adjacent to the Add to Cart button, showing quantity as a small number between the buttons. I'm reworking the quantity controls to display [-] [Add to cart] [+] with quantity shown in aria-label.

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here is a concise picture based on your repo.

## What this project is

**`my-spa-project`** is a **single-page application (SPA)** built for what looks like a **SZABIST TM Labs (SP26)** exercise. In the UI it behaves like a **small marketing site for a fresh-juice / “treat yourself” brand**: hero, menu-style sections, product cards, an **About** page with a short “Fruity Story” (mentions Karachi), and a **cart counter** in the nav that goes up when you “add to cart,” with a **success toast** when an item is added.

So it is both a **branded landing experience** and a **vehicle for practicing React concepts** (state, routing, components, and a UI library).

## What you are using (stack)

| Area | Technology |
|------|------------|
| **UI library** | **React 19** (`react`, `react-dom`) |
| **Build / dev** | **Vite 7** (`vite`, `@vitejs/plugin-react`) — dev server, bundling, `npm run dev` / `build` / `preview` |
| **Routing** | **React Router 7** (`react-router-dom`) — `BrowserRouter`, `Routes`, routes for `/` and `/about` |
| **Component library** | **MUI (Material UI) 7** — e.g. `Snackbar`, `Alert`, `Button`; **@mui/icons-material** for icons |
| **MUI styling** | **Emotion** (`@emotion/react`, `@emotion/styled`) — what MUI uses under the hood |
| **Custom styling** | **Plain CSS** — e.g. `Home.css`, `App.css`, `Navbar.css`, `index.css` for layout and the “quirky” look |
| **Quality** | **ESLint 9** with React Hooks and React Refresh plugins |
| **Language** | **JavaScript (JSX)** — `.jsx` files; TypeScript is only via **`@types/react`** for editor/tooling, not TS source |

In short: **React + Vite + React Router + MUI + Emotion + custom CSS**, aimed at a lab-style SPA with navigation, local state (cart count), and MUI feedback (snackbar).

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Your idea fits this kind of course well: **closed-book exams rarely test “can you build a SPA from zero” as fairly as “here is our lab codebase—now change it correctly.”** That matches how real work happens (read, understand, modify) and reduces memorization of trivia.

## What works well about “code given + modifications”

- **Tests understanding**, not recall of boilerplate (Vite config, every import).
- **Scales to time**: you can mark “does it run?” plus a short rubric per part.
- **Pairs with your project**: routing, `useState`, props, MUI, CSS classes you already taught.

**Risks to manage**

- **Copy-paste between variants**: solve with **parallel tasks** (same difficulty, different wording/small differences: e.g. change color vs change text, `/about` vs `/contact`).
- **Ambiguity**: every question needs **one observable outcome** (“navbar shows X”, “clicking Y does Z”).
- **Setup time**: students need the **same starter zip** and clear “do not rename files unless asked.”

## Suggested plan for “4 papers” (A vs B, 2 variants each)

Think of it as a **2×2 grid**:

| | **Variant 1** | **Variant 2** |
|--|----------------|---------------|
| **Paper A** (e.g. Section A / Midterm) | A1 | A2 |
| **Paper B** (e.g. Section B / or “Set B”) | B1 | B2 |

- **A vs B**: same **skills and marks**, **different** details (labels, routes, colors, component names if you want).  
- **1 vs 2**: **alternate wording** and **non-overlapping edits** so neighbors cannot copy line-for-line.

Each paper might be **3–5 tasks**, **60–90 minutes**, **100 marks** split by weight (e.g. 20 + 25 + 25 + 30).

## Task types that map cleanly to *your* project

1. **React state / props** — e.g. “Add a second counter” or “Pass a callback from `App` to `Home` and call it from a button.”  
2. **React Router** — e.g. “Add a route `/menu` and a minimal `Menu.jsx`” or “Change `/about` link text and page title.”  
3. **MUI** — e.g. “Replace native button with MUI `Button` with given `sx`” or “Change `Snackbar` position / duration.”  
4. **Conditional UI** — e.g. “Show ‘Cart is empty’ when count is 0.”  
5. **CSS** — e.g. “Change `.hero-section` background” or “Add a media query for mobile.”  
6. **Small component** — e.g. “Extract a `Footer` fragment into `Footer.jsx` and import it.”

Mix **easy verification** (visual + console) with **one** slightly harder integration task per paper.

## What I would avoid (or use sparingly)

- Huge refactors or “rewrite in TypeScript” in a short exam unless you’ve drilled it.  
- Questions that require internet/docs unless open-book is allowed.  
- Identical instructions on A1 and B1 except one word—students will still share; **change the actual code paths** (different class names or routes).

## Practical packaging

- **One starter project** (or two: “base” + “with intentional bug” for a debug question).  
- **Printed or PDF brief** with: folder name, Node version note, `npm install` / `npm run dev`, and **exact filenames** to touch.  
- **Marking sheet**: per task—runs (Y/N), requirement checklist, minor penalties for side bugs.

---

**Bottom line:** Your plan is sound. I’d formalize it as **parallel papers (A/B) × two scrambled variants**, each built from **small, observable edits** aligned to `App.jsx`, `Home.jsx`, `About.jsx`, `Navbar.jsx`, `ProductCard.jsx`, and `Home.css`.

If you want the next step, I can **draft all four papers** (A1, A2, B1, B2) with specific tasks, marks, and starter snippets—tell me your **exam duration**, **total marks**, and whether **Section A and B** are different cohorts or different topics (e.g. midterm vs final).

Here is what was added and how it is structured.

## File created

**`exam/MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md`**

It includes:

1. **Invigilator / marking notes** — shared rubric (5 questions × **4 marks** = **20**), fairness note, optional rules for notes/docs.
2. **A reusable cover block** — Section **6A / 6B**, Set **I / II**, roll number, name, 90 minutes, 20 marks.
3. **Four full papers:**
   - **Section 6A — Set I** (`/offers`, Router import debug, local `visitCount`, Snackbar, `.navbar` color)
   - **Section 6A — Set II** (`/faq`, `onClick={handleClick()}` debug, clamped `rating`, ProductCard MUI, hero `min-height`)
   - **Section 6B — Set I** (`/contact`, missing `key` in `map`, controlled `feedback` input, Alert severity, `.promo-strip` padding)
   - **Section 6B — Set II** (`/deals`, default **export** debug, local `savedForLater`, About MUI button `sx`, `.hamburger` hover)

## Balance across all four

- Same **pattern**: **Q1** Debug · **Q2** State · **Q3** Router + new page + **`Link`** · **Q4** MUI · **Q5** CSS.
- **Difficulty**: Small trade-offs only (e.g. **controlled input** vs **clamped counter** vs **export** vs **import** debug) so no paper is clearly “short.”
- **6B Set II Q2** was revised: it was originally **lifted state** in `App` + `Home` (heavier). It now matches the others: **local state in `About.jsx`** only.

## Your workflow

- Print **one section + one set** per student (e.g. only “Section **6A** — **Set I**” pages).
- Keep **Set I** and **Set II** as two versions within the same section to limit copying.
- Optional closing note in the file: fixing **`Navbar`** `<a href="/about">` → **`<Link>`** is praise-only, not required.

If you want, we can add a **one-page quick rubric** (checklist only) or **starter ZIP instructions** (which files to pre-break for practice).

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here is what was added.

## Word files (in `exam/`)

| File | Contents |
|------|----------|
| `exam/Midterm_Section_6A_Set_I.docx` | Section **6A**, **Set I** |
| `exam/Midterm_Section_6A_Set_II.docx` | Section **6A**, **Set II** |
| `exam/Midterm_Section_6B_Set_I.docx` | Section **6B**, **Set I** |
| `exam/Midterm_Section_6B_Set_II.docx` | Section **6B**, **Set II** |

Each file has the shared cover (instructions, roll no., 90 min / 20 marks) plus **only** that section’s five questions. Code appears in **Consolas** where it matters.

## Regenerating after edits

1. Update the text in `exam/generate-word-papers.mjs` (or keep `MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md` as the master and copy changes into the script when you change the exam).
2. Run:

```bash
npm run exam:word
```

(or `node exam/generate-word-papers.mjs`).

## Dependency

The **`docx`** package is a **devDependency** so Node can build `.docx` files. It does not affect the Vite app bundle.

If you want, we can next **sync the script from the markdown file automatically** so you only maintain one source.

---

_**User**_

i guess the papers are written on the point and difficult to understand for students. Can you do it a bit explanatory and in simple language.

---

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here is what changed.

### 1. **`exam/MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md`**
- **Student cover** is rewritten in plain language (“Before you start — read this”).
- **Every question** now has three layers:
  - **What this question checks** — what skill you are assessing  
  - **In simple words** — short explanation of ideas (imports, `onClick`, `key`, controlled input, `Link`, default export, etc.)  
  - **What you have to do** — numbered, concrete steps  
- **Hints** stay factual (e.g. Router import, `onClick` timing) without giving the final answer.
- **Teacher-only** bits are labeled (“Notes for teachers”, “Equivalence summary”, optional navbar note).

### 2. **`exam/generate-word-papers.mjs`**
- Matches the same structure, with small **Heading 3** blocks (“What this question checks”, “In simple words”, “What you have to do”).
- **Output folder:** files are written to **`exam/word/`** so Word does not lock old copies in `exam/`. Run `npm run exam:word` after edits to refresh.

### 3. **New Word files**
Regenerated at:

- `exam/word/Midterm_Section_6A_Set_I.docx`  
- `exam/word/Midterm_Section_6A_Set_II.docx`  
- `exam/word/Midterm_Section_6B_Set_I.docx`  
- `exam/word/Midterm_Section_6B_Set_II.docx`  

Older `.docx` files in `exam/` (if any) are unchanged; you can delete them or replace them with copies from `exam/word/`.

The **tasks and marks** are the same as before—only clarity and wording improved. If you want the generator to **overwrite** `exam/*.docx` again instead of `exam/word/`, say so and we can switch it back (close Word first to avoid file locks).

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: node -e "
const fs = require('fs');
const p = 'd:/Teaching/SZABIST/SP26/TM_Labs/my-spa-project/exam/MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md';
let s = fs.readFileSync(p, 'utf8');
s = s.replace(/\*\*In simple words:\*\*/g, '**Brief:**');
s = s.split('\n').filter(line => !/^\*\*What this question checks:\*\*/.test(line)).join('\n');
fs.writeFileSync(p, s);
console.log('Updated markdown');
"</summary>

```bash
node -e "
const fs = require('fs');

s = s.split('\n').filter(line => !/^\*\*What this question checks:\*\*/.test(line)).join('\n');
fs.writeFileSync(p, s);
console.log('Updated markdown');
"
```

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here is what changed.

### Section 6B — new focus on **`isOpen`** (balanced with 6A)

| | **6B Set I** | **6B Set II** |
|---|----------------|----------------|
| **Q1 (Debug)** | Wrong toggle: `onClick={() => setIsOpen(isOpen)}` — does not flip open/closed. Fix: use `setIsOpen(!isOpen)` (same idea as your lab hamburger). | Wrong `useState`: `const [isOpen] = useState(false)` — **no `setIsOpen`**. Fix: full destructuring `const [isOpen, setIsOpen] = useState(false)`. |
| **Q2 (State)** | **About:** simple **Like** counter (`likes`, one button) — clearer than the old controlled input. | **Navbar:** show **Menu: open** / **Menu: closed** next to the hamburger using **`isOpen`** (ternary). Keeps Q2 tied to `isOpen` without repeating Q1. |
| **Q3–Q5** | Unchanged: Contact + route, Alert, `.promo-strip` | Unchanged: Deals + route, MUI About button, hamburger CSS |

### Clarity

- A short **“How this paper is laid out”** line at the top of each 6B paper (what Q1–Q5 cover).
- **Q1** is a **single, concrete bug** with a short **Brief** and two clear tasks (explain + fix).
- **Q2 Set I** is a **one-pattern** task (counter only), similar difficulty to 6A Set I’s visit counter.
- **Q2 Set II** is **read and display state** in the navbar, paired with **Q1** on `useState` / `isOpen`.

### Files updated

- `exam/MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md` — full 6B text + equivalence table  
- `exam/generate-word-papers.mjs` — same content + **`exam/word-b/`** always gets the latest **6B** `.docx` first (so you still get new files if **`exam/word`** is locked in Word)

### Word files

- **`exam/word-b/Midterm_Section_6B_Set_I.docx`** and **`Midterm_Section_6B_Set_II.docx`** were written successfully.
- **`exam/word/`** could not overwrite the 6B files (likely open in Word). Close those two files and run:

`npm run exam:word`

Then **`exam/word/`** will match, or you can **copy from `exam/word-b/`** into **`exam/word/`**.

**6A papers** are unchanged in substance; only re-exported when you run the script.

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here’s what changed: papers are **a bit harder**, and the **wording is more detailed** (longer Briefs, clearer numbering, extra sub-parts, and a time-management note).

### Cover (all papers)
- Read each question **fully**; **all** listed parts count for full marks.
- Run the app, click through changes, fix **console** errors when possible.
- **Time tip:** ~**18 minutes per question**; if stuck, skip and return.

### Difficulty bumps (still balanced across the four papers)

| Area | What got harder |
|------|------------------|
| **Q1 Debug** | Extra short answer: **symptom** (6A I), **render-loop risk** (6A II), **two-click trace** + why `!isOpen` (6B I), **what `useState` returns** (6B II) |
| **Q2 State** | **6A I:** cap **99** + **Reset** · **6A II:** **Reset** to 5 · **6B I:** **Like** + **Unlike** (min 0) · **6B II:** **Menu** text + **`aria-expanded`** + **`aria-label="Toggle menu"`** |
| **Q3 Router** | **6A I:** **`<h2>`** “Limited-time picks” · **6A II:** **`<h2>`** “Quick answers” · **6B I:** **email + phone** · **6B II:** **`<ul>` / `<li>`** deals |
| **Q4 MUI** | **6A I:** **`Alert`** **`variant="filled"`** · **6A II:** **`borderWidth: 2px`** · **6B I:** **`filled`** + message · **6B II:** **`fontWeight: 700`** in **`sx`** (required) |
| **Q5 CSS** | **6A I:** **`transition`** on **`.section-title`** · **6A II:** **`.juice-card:hover`** shadow · **6B I:** **`border-top`** on footer · **6B II:** **`transition`** on **`.nav-links a`** + **`:hover`** |

### Files updated
- **`exam/MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md`** — full text + teacher marking table + equivalence table  
- **`exam/generate-word-papers.mjs`** — matches the above  
- **`.docx`** regenerated under **`exam/word/`** (and **`exam/word-b/`** for 6B backup)

If **90 minutes** feels tight with the extra parts, you can either **add 10–15 minutes** or treat one sub-part (e.g. Q1 “symptom” sentence) as **bonus**—say if you want that spelled out on the cover.

---

_**User**_

I said, i want a little detailed brief. you still provided the on the point brief.

---

Here’s what changed:

### 1. **`exam/MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md` — longer “Brief” blocks**
Every **Brief** is now **2–4 short paragraphs** (not a single line). They spell out:

- **What the idea is** (imports, `useState`, Router, MUI, CSS, `isOpen`, ARIA, etc.)
- **Why it matters** in a real page or for debugging  
- **How it ties to your lab** (without giving away answers)

**What you have to do** is unchanged in substance; only the **Brief** got richer.

**Teachers:** a note was added that **marks come from “What you have to do”** — the Brief is **context only**.

### 2. **`exam/generate-word-papers.mjs` — Briefs in Word**
Each question’s **Brief** in the `.docx` is now **several paragraphs** (aligned with the markdown), not one short line.

### 3. **Output folders**
- **`exam/word-latest/`** — all **four** papers are **always** written here first (so you still get files if **`exam/word`** is locked).
- Then **`exam/word/`** and **`exam/word-b/`** when possible.

Run **`npm run exam:word`** after edits; if Word has files open, use **`exam/word-latest/`** for the newest build.

If you want **even longer** Briefs (e.g. a short “Common mistakes” line per question), say which section(s) to extend first.

_**User**_

what does it mean destructure ?

---

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


**Destructuring** means: take **values out of** something structured (usually an **array** or an **object**) and put them into **separate variables** in one step—instead of writing many separate index or property lookups.

### Array destructuring (what you use with `useState`)

`useState` returns an **array of two things**: `[currentValue, setter]`.

**Without** destructuring you might write:

```js
const pair = useState(false);
const isOpen = pair[0];
const setIsOpen = pair[1];
```

**With** destructuring you write the same idea shorter:

```js
const [isOpen, setIsOpen] = useState(false);
```

So “destructure” here means: **pull out** the first element as `isOpen` and the second as `setIsOpen` from the array `useState` returns.

If you only write:

```js
const [isOpen] = useState(false);
```

you are **only** destructuring the **first** element. You **never** take the second, so you don’t get `setIsOpen`—that’s what your exam question about the bug is testing.

### Object destructuring (common elsewhere)

```js
const user = { name: "A", age: 20 };
const { name, age } = user;
```

Here you **pull** `name` and `age` out of `user` into variables with the same names.

---

**Short definition:** **Destructuring = unpacking** arrays or objects into variables in one assignment.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Analyzing exam papers**

I'm assessing the relative difficulty of exam questions across 4 papers to ensure balanced challenge levels. I'm evaluating question difficulty based on component complexity and implementation patterns across exam papers. I'm noting UI complexity differences between questions, especially Q4's ProductCard variant and Q5's hover rule additions. I'm identifying patterns in UI component variations across questions, particularly noting how Q4's ProductCard differs from other variants. I'm noting that Q1 part 2 might be too easy since the answer is nearly given, while Q2's Navbar with ARIA attributes presents a harder accessibility challenge. I'm identifying imbalance in question difficulty where Q1 part 2 in Set II is overly easy and Q4 in Sets I differ in complexity despite both touching Alert components. 

I'm noting that 6B Set II Q2 is significantly harder than other Q2s due to its combination of state management, accessibility, and Navbar components, making it the least fair question. 

**Reviewing exam questions**

I'm adjusting the wording of Q2 to avoid giving away the complete `useState` implementation in the rubric. </details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here is what was done and what you should do locally.

### Fix applied (fairness)
**Section 6B — Set II — Question 1** no longer prints the full solution. Item 2 now asks students to **write the correct full line** that declares **both** the state and the setter from `useState(false)`, without giving `const [isOpen, setIsOpen] = useState(false);` in the instructions.

- Updated: `exam/MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md`
- Updated: `exam/generate-word-papers.mjs` (Word script line for that item)

### Regenerating Word
`npm run exam:word` wrote the first three `.docx` files, then failed with **`EBUSY`** on `exam/word-latest/Midterm_Section_6B_Set_II.docx` (file almost certainly open in Word or locked). **Close Word (or that file)** and run:

`npm run exam:word`

so **6B Set II** and any copies to `exam/word/` / `exam/word-b/` complete.

### Balance reminder (unchanged)
**6B Set II Q2** is still a bit heavier (ternary + `aria-expanded` + `aria-label` in `Navbar.jsx`) than some other papers’ Q2; that is fine if you use **partial credit** or accept slightly more work on that one.

If you want, we can next tweak 6B Set II Q2 wording only (no skill change) to align perceived load with the others.

import zipfile, re, os
from xml.etree import ElementTree as ET

ns = {'w': 'http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main'}

def docx_text(path):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        xml = z.read('word/document.xml')
    root = ET.fromstring(xml)
    paras = []
    for p in root.iter('{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}p'):
        texts = []
        for t in p.iter('{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}t'):
            if t.text:
                texts.append(t.text)
            if t.tail:
                texts.append(t.tail)
        line = ''.join(texts).strip()
        if line:
            paras.append(line)
    return '\n'.join(paras)

base = r'd:\Teaching\SZABIST\SP26\Exams\Mid Term'
for folder, name in [('6A', 'Paper-A'), ('6A', 'Paper-B'), ('6B', 'Paper-A'), ('6B', 'Paper-B')]:
    p = os.path.join(base, folder, f'Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6{name[0]}{name[1]} {name}.docx'.replace('6Paper', '6'))

```bash
        xml = z.read('word/document.xml')
    root = ET.fromstring(xml)

    for p in root.iter('{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}p'):
```

import zipfile, os
from xml.etree import ElementTree as ET

def docx_text(path):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        xml = z.read('word/document.xml')
    root = ET.fromstring(xml)
    paras = []
    w = '{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}'
    for p in root.iter(w + 'p'):
        texts = []
        for t in p.iter(w + 't'):
            if t.text:
                texts.append(t.text)
            if t.tail:
                texts.append(t.tail)
        line = ''.join(texts).strip()
        if line:
            paras.append(line)
    return '\n'.join(paras)

base = r'd:\Teaching\SZABIST\SP26\Exams\Mid Term'
files = [
    r'6A\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6A Paper-A.docx',
    r'6A\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6A Paper-B.docx',
    r'6B\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6B Paper-A.docx',
    r'6B\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6B Paper-B.docx',
]
for rel in files:
    p = os.path.join(base, rel)
    print('='*70)
    print(rel)
    print('='*70)
    print(docx_text(p))
    print()
"

import zipfile, os, sys
from xml.etree import ElementTree as ET

def docx_text(path):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        xml = z.read('word/document.xml')
    root = ET.fromstring(xml)
    paras = []
    w = '{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}'
    for p in root.iter(w + 'p'):
        texts = []
        for t in p.iter(w + 't'):

            if t.tail:
                texts.append(t.tail)
        line = ''.join(texts).strip()
        if line:
            paras.append(line)
    return '\n'.join(paras)

files = [
    r'6A\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6A Paper-A.docx',
    r'6A\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6A Paper-B.docx',
    r'6B\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6B Paper-A.docx',

```bash
import zipfile, os, sys
from xml.etree import ElementTree as ET

def docx_text(path):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        xml = z.read('word/document.xml')
    root = ET.fromstring(xml)
    paras = []
    w = '{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}'
    for p in root.iter(w + 'p'):
        texts = []
        for t in p.iter(w + 't'):
            if t.text:
                texts.append(t.text)
            if t.tail:
                texts.append(t.tail)
        line = ''.join(texts).strip()
        if line:
            paras.append(line)
    return '\n'.join(paras)

base = r'd:\Teaching\SZABIST\SP26\Exams\Mid Term'
files = [
    r'6A\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6A Paper-A.docx',
    r'6A\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6A Paper-B.docx',
    r'6B\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6B Paper-A.docx',
    r'6B\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6B Paper-B.docx',
]
out = []

    p = os.path.join(base, rel)
    out.append('='*70 + '\n' + rel + '\n' + '='*70 + '\n')
```

import zipfile, os
from xml.etree import ElementTree as ET

def docx_text(path):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        xml = z.read('word/document.xml')
    root = ET.fromstring(xml)
    paras = []
    w = '{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}'

        for t in p.iter(w + 't'):
            if t.text:
                texts.append(t.text)
            if t.tail:
                texts.append(t.tail)
        line = ''.join(texts).strip()
        if line:
            paras.append(line)
    return '\n'.join(paras)

```bash
$env:PYTHONIOENCODING='utf-8'; python -c "
import zipfile, os
from xml.etree import ElementTree as ET

def docx_text(path):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        xml = z.read('word/document.xml')
    root = ET.fromstring(xml)
    paras = []
    w = '{http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main}'
    for p in root.iter(w + 'p'):
        texts = []
        for t in p.iter(w + 't'):
            if t.text:
                texts.append(t.text)
            if t.tail:
                texts.append(t.tail)
        line = ''.join(texts).strip()
        if line:
            paras.append(line)
    return '\n'.join(paras)

base = r'd:\Teaching\SZABIST\SP26\Exams\Mid Term'
files = [
    r'6A\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6A Paper-A.docx',
    r'6A\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6A Paper-B.docx',
    r'6B\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6B Paper-A.docx',
    r'6B\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6B Paper-B.docx',
]
out = []
for rel in files:
    p = os.path.join(base, rel)
    out.append('='*70 + chr(10) + rel + chr(10) + '='*70 + chr(10))
    out.append(docx_text(p) + chr(10)*2)
wp = r'd:\Teaching\SZABIST\SP26\TM_Labs\my-spa-project\exam_extracted.txt'
open(wp, 'w', encoding='utf-8').write(''.join(out))
print('Wrote', wp)
"
```

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


I pulled the text from all four files in those folders:

- `6A\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6A Paper-A.docx`
- `6A\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6A Paper-B.docx`
- `6B\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6B Paper-A.docx`
- `6B\Mid Term Question Paper - Section 6B Paper-B.docx`

Here is a concise balance/fairness review and concrete fixes.

---

### Critical wording error (all four papers)

Every paper says: **“Ensure all necessary using directives are included.”**  
That phrase is **C# / .NET** (“using” directives). For this React/JS course it should be something like **“Ensure all necessary `import` statements are included.”** (or “imports” only). I would change this on all four documents.

---

### Fairness: **6B Paper-B — Question 4**

The paper still **prints the full solution**:

- “**Correct line: `const [isOpen, setIsOpen] = useState(false);`**”

Anyone can copy that line without understanding destructuring, so **Paper-B is easier on Q4** than the other papers’ debugging/state questions. **Remove that line** and ask only for the correct full line (same idea as in your repo’s `MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md` after the earlier fix).

---

### Smaller issues

1. **6A Paper-B — Question 4**  
   In the Word file, **“Question # 4(04 Marks)”** runs straight into **“onClick”** with **no space** (`...Marks)onClick`). Fix spacing/formatting for readability.

2. **6B Paper-B — Question 3**  
   Example text **“Saved for later: {5}”** is easy to misread as “always show 5.” Prefer **“Saved for later: {savedForLater}”** or an example like **“Saved for later: 3”** (numeric example, not `{5}` as if it were JSX).

3. **6B Paper-B — Question 1**  
   Starts with **“On About.jsx…”** but **Question # 1** heading doesn’t appear before that line in the extract (layout may have merged cells). Worth checking in Word so **“Question # 1 (04 Marks)”** clearly precedes the stem.

---

### Balance (are all four “equally tough”?)

They are **close** in overall mix (each has: small bug/theory, React state or UI, routing + `Link`, MUI or App tweak, CSS). **Exact** parity is impossible, but these are the **main asymmetries**:

| Area | Note |
|------|------|
| **6B Paper-A Q1** | Logic bug (`setIsOpen` toggle) — conceptual + one line of code. |
| **6B Paper-B Q1** | MUI `Button` `size` / `fullWidth` / `sx` — different skill (styling API), not a “bug fix.” Same marks, **different perceived difficulty** for different students. |
| **6A Paper-A Q1** | Explain + fix **import** — two parts. |
| **6A Paper-B Q1** | Build **FAQ** + route + link — no separate “explain the bug” question; **different shape** from 6A-A. |

None of this is “wrong,” but if you want **tighter fairness**, consider either: parallel **Q1 types** across sets (e.g. both 6B papers start with a **Navbar / `isOpen`** task), or accept the mix and **marking guidance** (e.g. rubric notes for MUI vs toggle).

After you **remove the Q4 spoiler** on **6B Paper-B**, the biggest objective unfairness is addressed. Fixing **“using directives”** removes a **factual** mistake that can confuse students.

---

### Files to edit

Only these finalized Word files (outside the repo):

- `d:\Teaching\SZABIST\SP26\Exams\Mid Term\6A\` — both `.docx`
- `d:\Teaching\SZABIST\SP26\Exams\Mid Term\6B\` — both `.docx`

If you want the **same** wording as your teaching copy, sync **6B Paper-B Q4** with `exam\MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md` and regenerate, or patch the `.docx` manually in Word.

I can’t edit those paths from the workspace tools reliably; say if you want a **short replacement paragraph** for Q4 and the instruction line to paste into Word.

---

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here is a practical split for **sharing with students** vs **keeping private**, and how it fits **“build from the start”** vs **“exam starter with bugs”**.

---

### Never share (cheating risk or teacher-only)

| Item | Why |
|------|-----|
| **`exam/`** (all of it: `.md`, `generate-word-papers.mjs`, Word sources, rubrics) | Questions, variants, and your internal notes. |
| **Any folder with “answer key”, solutions, or your full finished lab repo** | Direct copy-paste of working code. |
| **Private repos / solution branches** | Same as above. |
| **`.cursor/`, internal tooling, unpublished slides with full code** | If it mirrors the exam solution. |

Students do **not** need `docx` tooling in `package.json` (`exam:word`, `docx` devDependency) unless you explicitly teach that; strip it from a student `package.json`.

---

### Safe to share (tooling + shell only)

These are **not** the assignment solution; they only define the stack and how the app boots.

- **`package.json`** — dependencies: `react`, `react-dom`, `react-router-dom`, `@mui/material`, `@emotion/react`, `@emotion/styled`, `@mui/icons-material` (if you use icons), Vite + `@vitejs/plugin-react`, ESLint deps if you use them. Remove teacher-only scripts/deps.
- **`package-lock.json`** *(optional)* — speeds `npm ci`; students can also run `npm install` from `package.json` alone.
- **`vite.config.js`**
- **`index.html`**
- **`public/`** (e.g. `vite.svg`) if referenced
- **`eslint.config.js`** *(if you want linting in the exam machine)*
- **`src/main.jsx`** — standard `createRoot` + `App` import
- **`src/index.css`** — global reset/fonts only (no exam-specific “clever” layout that answers Q5)

That is enough for **“from zero”** if the exam is **only** “create these files and wire routes” with **no** pre-supplied buggy components.

---

### Midterm reality: your papers reference **existing file names** (`Navbar.jsx`, `About.jsx`, `ProductCard.jsx`, `App.jsx`, CSS files)

So you have two honest options:

1. **True greenfield**  
   Share only the list above + **instructions** (“create `src/components/Navbar.jsx`”, etc.).  
   **Risk:** wording on the paper must match (or you accept any equivalent structure).

2. **Exam starter (recommended for in-lab / debugging questions)**  
   Share a **small zip** that looks like the lab **structure** but is **not** your final GitHub project:
   - Include **stub or intentionally buggy** `App.jsx`, `Navbar.jsx`, `About.jsx`, `ProductCard.jsx`, `Home.jsx`, and minimal CSS so the app **runs** (or runs with the bugs you printed on the paper).
   - **Remove** extra features, comments that teach the answer, and anything not needed for the paper.
   - **Do not** include complete correct routing/Snackbar/MUI fixes if those are what you’re assessing.

That way they **cannot** cheat from the “real” lab repo, but they still have the **right filenames and skeleton** the questions assume.

---

### Concrete “student bundle” checklist

**Include**

- Config: `package.json` (clean), `vite.config.js`, `index.html`, `public/*` as needed, `src/main.jsx`, baseline `src/index.css`.
- Either **minimal empty** `App.jsx` + “you build the rest”, **or** **exam starter** components/CSS as above.

**Exclude**

- Entire **`exam/`** tree  
- **`generate-word-papers.mjs`**, teacher markdown papers  
- Full **`Home.css` / `Navbar.css`** if they contain **finished** styles for every question (unless you only need a subset and strip the rest)  
- **`.git`** if it points to a repo that has solutions (or use a **fresh folder** with no history)

---

### Optional hygiene

- Give a **single zip** name like `midterm-starter-sp26.zip` with a **README** that only says Node version, `npm install`, `npm run dev` — **no** solution hints.
- If you use a **lockfile**, generate it **from the trimmed** `package.json` so `npm ci` matches.

If you tell me whether the exam is **fully greenfield** or **buggy starter**, I can list **exact paths** from your repo to copy vs delete line-by-line (still without creating new markdown files unless you ask).

---

_**User**_

also, the files that you think I should share. copy all those files in a folder named sharedFIles.

---

Here is what is in place.

### Folder: `sharedFIles` (at the project root)

| Item | Purpose |
|------|--------|
| `package.json` | Same stack as your app (React, MUI, Router, Vite, ESLint). **No** `exam:word` or `docx`. |
| `package-lock.json` | Lockfile for reproducible installs (from `npm install --package-lock-only`). |
| `vite.config.js` | Vite + React plugin |
| `index.html` | Entry HTML + Poppins font link (title set to **Web Engineering — Starter**) |
| `eslint.config.js` | Same ESLint flat config as the main project |
| `.gitignore` | Standard ignores + **`node_modules`** |
| `public/vite.svg` | Favicon asset |
| `src/main.jsx` | Bootstraps React |
| `src/index.css` | Minimal global CSS (box-sizing + body) |
| `src/App.jsx` | Tiny placeholder so **`npm run dev`** works |
| `READ_ME_FIRST.txt` | Short note: run **`npm install`**, do **not** zip **`node_modules`** |

**Not copied** (on purpose): `App.jsx` / `Navbar` / `Home` / `ProductCard` / `About` / `Home.css` / `exam/` — so this stays a **starter**, not your lab solution.

### `node_modules` under `sharedFIles`

`npm` created **`sharedFIles/node_modules`** while generating the lockfile. On Windows, deleting it from here failed (file locks). **Before you zip this for students:**

1. Close anything using that folder (IDE, terminals, antivirus scan).
2. Delete **`sharedFIles\node_modules`** in Explorer or:  
   `rmdir /s /q sharedFIles\node_modules`

Students should run **`npm install`** after unzipping. **`package-lock.json`** is included so installs stay consistent.

### Repo tweak

**`.gitignore`** now includes **`sharedFIles/node_modules`** so that folder is not committed if it remains locally.

If you want the folder renamed to **`sharedFiles`** (correct spelling), say so and we can align the name.

---

_**User**_

see, the students will create the vite project from the start. I want you to tell me only the jsx and css files which I should share. Let's do one thing you create a copy of those files which should be shared in a new folder named starterPack and modify those files and remove the code which you think will help them for their questions. Got it ?

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here is what is in **`starterPack/`** and how it differs from your full project.

### Files in `starterPack`

| File | Role |
|------|------|
| `App.jsx` | Router + Snackbar + routes. Snackbar message is generic **"Item added."** (not the long lab string). `autoHideDuration` / `anchorOrigin` / `Alert` **severity** are still “lab defaults” so students can change them in the exam. |
| `index.css` | Same global reset as `src/index.css`. |
| `Home.css` | **Full** copy of your `Home.css` (layout and visuals preserved). |
| `pages/Home.jsx` | Same as your page; **`import React` removed** (not needed with the modern JSX runtime). |
| `pages/About.jsx` | **MUI `Button` has no `sx`** (no size, `minWidth`, or colors—those stay for the exam). |
| `components/Navbar.jsx` | **`import React` removed**; About uses **`Link`** (SPA). Toggle / `useState` left **working** so the menu still opens. |
| `components/Navbar.css` | **`cursor: pointer` removed** from `.hamburger` so “add cursor / hover / transition” style questions are not already done. |
| `components/ProductCard.jsx` | **Stripped**: no `ShoppingCartIcon`, **no `sx`** (colors, radius, hover). Plain **`variant="contained"`** and a simple `onClick` so outline/red variants and handler-style questions stay open. |
| `READ_ME.txt` | Where to paste files + reminder about **`src/assets/img/`** and Vite setup. |

### Not in the pack (by design)

- **`main.jsx`**, **`index.html`**, **`package.json`**, **`vite.config.js`** — students create the Vite app themselves, as you wanted.
- **`App.css`** — not used in your app.
- **Assets** — `Home.jsx` / `Navbar.jsx` still **import** `../assets/img/...`; students (or you) must supply the **same image folder** as in the lab, or those imports will break.

### What this avoids “giving away”

- **Snackbar / Alert**: not set to typical exam targets (e.g. 4s, bottom-center, warning, exact message).
- **About `Button`**: no `sx` / `size` / `fullWidth` “solution”.
- **ProductCard**: no icon, no black/red **sx** styling.
- **Navbar CSS**: hamburger **cursor** not preset (you can add more stripping later if you want zero hints on nav-link transitions).

If you want **`Home.css`** shortened (only sections that appear on the exam) to reduce bulk or accidental hints, say so and we can trim it in a second pass.

---

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here is what was set up.

### New project folder

**`d:\Teaching\SZABIST\SP26\TM_Labs\my-spa-exam-handout`**

It is a **full copy** of your app (Vite config, `public/`, `src/`, `README.md`, ESLint, etc.) with:

- **`exam/`** excluded (teacher-only)
- **`sharedFIles/`**, **`starterPack/`** excluded
- **`node_modules/`**, **`dist/`** excluded (students run `npm install`)
- **`package-lock.json`** was regenerated after editing `package.json`
- **`src/assets/img/`** — copied in a second step (the first copy did not include `img`; **`28` image files** are there now)

### What was changed in the handout (so it does not “solve” the exam)

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| **`package.json`** | Removed `exam:word` script and **`docx`** devDependency. |
| **`src/App.jsx`** | Removed inline comments; Snackbar text is **"Item added."** (generic); **Snackbar / Alert** settings stay as simple defaults (not tuned to exam targets). |
| **`src/components/Navbar.jsx`** | Dropped `React` import; **About** is still **`<a href="/about">`** (not `Link`), so “use `Link` for SPA navigation” work is not done for them; **hamburger** still toggles correctly. |
| **`src/components/Navbar.css`** | Removed **`cursor: pointer`** from `.hamburger`. |
| **`src/pages/About.jsx`** | Removed **`sx`** on the MUI `Button` and the teaching comments. |
| **`src/components/ProductCard.jsx`** | Removed **`ShoppingCartIcon`**, **`sx`**, and commented “tutorial” blocks; **`Button`** is plain **`contained`** with a simple **`onClick`**. |
| **`src/pages/Home.jsx`** | Removed **`import React`**. |

### What you should do before sharing

1. From **`my-spa-exam-handout`**, run **`npm install`** (once) to confirm it runs.
2. Zip **`my-spa-exam-handout`** (without **`node_modules`**) for students.

Your **original** repo under **`my-spa-project`** is unchanged.

**Subject:** Midterm question papers and exam handout — Web Engineering (Sections 6A & 6B)

Dear [Program Manager’s name],

I am sharing the midterm question papers for **Web Engineering**, along with the **lab handout** students must use during the exam.

**Question papers**  
Each section has **two variants**: **Paper A** and **Paper B** (so students in the same section are not all on identical wording). The sets are organized for **Section 6A** and **Section 6B** as discussed.

**Exam format**  
The assessment is **lab-based**: students work in the development environment on tasks tied to our React / Vite SPA work.

**Student materials**  
Students will need the attached archive **`my-spa-exam-handout.zip`** (or the filename you use when you distribute it). This package contains the starter project they are expected to open and modify during the exam. Please ensure this file is distributed to students **before or at** the exam session, per your usual process.

**Attachments**  
- Question papers (Sections **6A** and **6B**, **A** and **B** variants as applicable)  
- **`my-spa-exam-handout.zip`** — exam handout project  

If you need anything else for scheduling, printing, or LMS upload, let me know.

Kind regards,  
[Your name]  
[Title — e.g. Course Instructor, Web Engineering]  
[Contact if you use one]

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here is a concise picture based on your repo.

## What this project is

**`my-spa-project`** is a **single-page application (SPA)** built for what looks like a **SZABIST TM Labs (SP26)** exercise. In the UI it behaves like a **small marketing site for a fresh-juice / “treat yourself” brand**: hero, menu-style sections, product cards, an **About** page with a short “Fruity Story” (mentions Karachi), and a **cart counter** in the nav that goes up when you “add to cart,” with a **success toast** when an item is added.

So it is both a **branded landing experience** and a **vehicle for practicing React concepts** (state, routing, components, and a UI library).

## What you are using (stack)

| Area | Technology |
|------|------------|
| **UI library** | **React 19** (`react`, `react-dom`) |
| **Build / dev** | **Vite 7** (`vite`, `@vitejs/plugin-react`) — dev server, bundling, `npm run dev` / `build` / `preview` |
| **Routing** | **React Router 7** (`react-router-dom`) — `BrowserRouter`, `Routes`, routes for `/` and `/about` |
| **Component library** | **MUI (Material UI) 7** — e.g. `Snackbar`, `Alert`, `Button`; **@mui/icons-material** for icons |
| **MUI styling** | **Emotion** (`@emotion/react`, `@emotion/styled`) — what MUI uses under the hood |
| **Custom styling** | **Plain CSS** — e.g. `Home.css`, `App.css`, `Navbar.css`, `index.css` for layout and the “quirky” look |
| **Quality** | **ESLint 9** with React Hooks and React Refresh plugins |
| **Language** | **JavaScript (JSX)** — `.jsx` files; TypeScript is only via **`@types/react`** for editor/tooling, not TS source |

In short: **React + Vite + React Router + MUI + Emotion + custom CSS**, aimed at a lab-style SPA with navigation, local state (cart count), and MUI feedback (snackbar).

---

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Your idea fits this kind of course well: **closed-book exams rarely test “can you build a SPA from zero” as fairly as “here is our lab codebase—now change it correctly.”** That matches how real work happens (read, understand, modify) and reduces memorization of trivia.

## What works well about “code given + modifications”

- **Tests understanding**, not recall of boilerplate (Vite config, every import).
- **Scales to time**: you can mark “does it run?” plus a short rubric per part.
- **Pairs with your project**: routing, `useState`, props, MUI, CSS classes you already taught.

**Risks to manage**

- **Copy-paste between variants**: solve with **parallel tasks** (same difficulty, different wording/small differences: e.g. change color vs change text, `/about` vs `/contact`).
- **Ambiguity**: every question needs **one observable outcome** (“navbar shows X”, “clicking Y does Z”).
- **Setup time**: students need the **same starter zip** and clear “do not rename files unless asked.”

## Suggested plan for “4 papers” (A vs B, 2 variants each)

Think of it as a **2×2 grid**:

| | **Variant 1** | **Variant 2** |
|--|----------------|---------------|
| **Paper A** (e.g. Section A / Midterm) | A1 | A2 |
| **Paper B** (e.g. Section B / or “Set B”) | B1 | B2 |

- **A vs B**: same **skills and marks**, **different** details (labels, routes, colors, component names if you want).  
- **1 vs 2**: **alternate wording** and **non-overlapping edits** so neighbors cannot copy line-for-line.

Each paper might be **3–5 tasks**, **60–90 minutes**, **100 marks** split by weight (e.g. 20 + 25 + 25 + 30).

## Task types that map cleanly to *your* project

1. **React state / props** — e.g. “Add a second counter” or “Pass a callback from `App` to `Home` and call it from a button.”  
2. **React Router** — e.g. “Add a route `/menu` and a minimal `Menu.jsx`” or “Change `/about` link text and page title.”  
3. **MUI** — e.g. “Replace native button with MUI `Button` with given `sx`” or “Change `Snackbar` position / duration.”  
4. **Conditional UI** — e.g. “Show ‘Cart is empty’ when count is 0.”  
5. **CSS** — e.g. “Change `.hero-section` background” or “Add a media query for mobile.”  
6. **Small component** — e.g. “Extract a `Footer` fragment into `Footer.jsx` and import it.”

Mix **easy verification** (visual + console) with **one** slightly harder integration task per paper.

## What I would avoid (or use sparingly)

- Huge refactors or “rewrite in TypeScript” in a short exam unless you’ve drilled it.  
- Questions that require internet/docs unless open-book is allowed.  
- Identical instructions on A1 and B1 except one word—students will still share; **change the actual code paths** (different class names or routes).

## Practical packaging

- **One starter project** (or two: “base” + “with intentional bug” for a debug question).  
- **Printed or PDF brief** with: folder name, Node version note, `npm install` / `npm run dev`, and **exact filenames** to touch.  
- **Marking sheet**: per task—runs (Y/N), requirement checklist, minor penalties for side bugs.

---

**Bottom line:** Your plan is sound. I’d formalize it as **parallel papers (A/B) × two scrambled variants**, each built from **small, observable edits** aligned to `App.jsx`, `Home.jsx`, `About.jsx`, `Navbar.jsx`, `ProductCard.jsx`, and `Home.css`.

If you want the next step, I can **draft all four papers** (A1, A2, B1, B2) with specific tasks, marks, and starter snippets—tell me your **exam duration**, **total marks**, and whether **Section A and B** are different cohorts or different topics (e.g. midterm vs final).

---

Here is what was added and how it is structured.

## File created

**`exam/MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md`**

It includes:

1. **Invigilator / marking notes** — shared rubric (5 questions × **4 marks** = **20**), fairness note, optional rules for notes/docs.
2. **A reusable cover block** — Section **6A / 6B**, Set **I / II**, roll number, name, 90 minutes, 20 marks.
3. **Four full papers:**
   - **Section 6A — Set I** (`/offers`, Router import debug, local `visitCount`, Snackbar, `.navbar` color)
   - **Section 6A — Set II** (`/faq`, `onClick={handleClick()}` debug, clamped `rating`, ProductCard MUI, hero `min-height`)
   - **Section 6B — Set I** (`/contact`, missing `key` in `map`, controlled `feedback` input, Alert severity, `.promo-strip` padding)
   - **Section 6B — Set II** (`/deals`, default **export** debug, local `savedForLater`, About MUI button `sx`, `.hamburger` hover)

## Balance across all four

- Same **pattern**: **Q1** Debug · **Q2** State · **Q3** Router + new page + **`Link`** · **Q4** MUI · **Q5** CSS.
- **Difficulty**: Small trade-offs only (e.g. **controlled input** vs **clamped counter** vs **export** vs **import** debug) so no paper is clearly “short.”
- **6B Set II Q2** was revised: it was originally **lifted state** in `App` + `Home` (heavier). It now matches the others: **local state in `About.jsx`** only.

## Your workflow

- Print **one section + one set** per student (e.g. only “Section **6A** — **Set I**” pages).
- Keep **Set I** and **Set II** as two versions within the same section to limit copying.
- Optional closing note in the file: fixing **`Navbar`** `<a href="/about">` → **`<Link>`** is praise-only, not required.

If you want, we can add a **one-page quick rubric** (checklist only) or **starter ZIP instructions** (which files to pre-break for practice).

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_


Here is what was added.

## Word files (in `exam/`)

| File | Contents |
|------|----------|
| `exam/Midterm_Section_6A_Set_I.docx` | Section **6A**, **Set I** |
| `exam/Midterm_Section_6A_Set_II.docx` | Section **6A**, **Set II** |
| `exam/Midterm_Section_6B_Set_I.docx` | Section **6B**, **Set I** |
| `exam/Midterm_Section_6B_Set_II.docx` | Section **6B**, **Set II** |

Each file has the shared cover (instructions, roll no., 90 min / 20 marks) plus **only** that section’s five questions. Code appears in **Consolas** where it matters.

## Regenerating after edits

1. Update the text in `exam/generate-word-papers.mjs` (or keep `MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md` as the master and copy changes into the script when you change the exam).
2. Run:

```bash
npm run exam:word
```

(or `node exam/generate-word-papers.mjs`).

## Dependency

The **`docx`** package is a **devDependency** so Node can build `.docx` files. It does not affect the Vite app bundle.

If you want, we can next **sync the script from the markdown file automatically** so you only maintain one source.

---

_**User**_

i guess the papers are written on the point and difficult to understand for students. Can you do it a bit explanatory and in simple language.

---

Here is what changed.

### 1. **`exam/MIDTERM_SPA_Sections_6A_6B.md`**
- **Student cover** is rewritten in plain language (“Before you start — read this”).
- **Every question** now has three layers:
  - **What this question checks** — what skill you are assessing  
  - **In simple words** — short explanation of ideas (imports, `onClick`, `key`, controlled input, `Link`, default export, etc.)  
  - **What you have to do** — numbered, concrete steps  
- **Hints** stay factual (e.g. Router import, `onClick` timing) without giving the final answer.
- **Teacher-only** bits are labeled (“Notes for teachers”, “Equivalence summary”, optional navbar note).

### 2. **`exam/generate-word-papers.mjs`**
- Matches the same structure, with small **Heading 3** blocks (“What this question checks”, “In simple words”, “What you have to do”).
- **Output folder:** files are written to **`exam/word/`** so Word does not lock old copies in `exam/`. Run `npm run exam:word` after edits to refresh.

### 3. **New Word files**
Regenerated at:

- `exam/word/Midterm_Section_6A_Set_I.docx`  
- `exam/word/Midterm_Section_6A_Set_II.docx`  
- `exam/word/Midterm_Section_6B_Set_I.docx`  
- `exam/word/Midterm_Section_6B_Set_II.docx`  

Older `.docx` files in `exam/` (if any) are unchanged; you can delete them or replace them with copies from `exam/word/`.

The **tasks and marks** are the same as before—only clarity and wording improved. If you want the generator to **overwrite** `exam/*.docx` again instead of `exam/word/`, say so and we can switch it back (close Word first to avoid file locks).

I'd be happy to help you create PowerPoint slides! However, it looks like you haven't shared the content yet. Could you please share the slides content you'd like me to use? You can paste the text, attach a file, or describe what you'd like on each slide.

---

_**User**_

Presentation Slides (The Theory & Vocabulary)
Slide 1: Title Slide

Title: Week 5: Version Control Fundamentals

Subtitle: Building the "Source" of our Pipeline (Git & GitHub)

Speaker Notes: "Welcome to the practical phase of the course! For the last four weeks, we talked about automated pipelines and cloud servers. But before we can automate our code, we need a secure, reliable place to store it. Today, we build the very first stage of the CI/CD pipeline: Source Control."

Slide 2: The "Final_v2_USE_THIS" Problem

Text:

How do you currently share group project code? USB drives? Emailing ZIP files?

What happens when two people edit the exact same file at the same time?

Result: Overwritten work, lost code, and total chaos.

Speaker Notes: "We've all been there. You name a file Project_Final, then Project_Final_v2, then Project_Real_Final_DO_NOT_TOUCH. This is a nightmare for professional software teams. We need a system that tracks exactly who changed what, and prevents team members from accidentally deleting each other's work."

Slide 3: What is Version Control? (The Time Machine)

Text:

A system that records changes to a file (or set of files) over time.

The Time Machine: You can instantly recall specific, older versions of your project.

The Sandbox: You can safely experiment without breaking the main application.

Speaker Notes: "Version control is like a time machine for your code. If you make a massive mistake and the app crashes, you don't have to spend five hours hitting 'undo' or trying to find the bug. You simply tell the system to roll back to the exact snapshot from yesterday when everything worked perfectly."

Slide 4: The Crucial Distinction: Git vs. GitHub

Text:

Git: The Engine. A command-line tool installed locally on your laptop that tracks file changes.

GitHub: The Cloud. A website where you host your Git repositories so the whole world (or your team) can see them.

Speaker Notes: "This is a guaranteed exam question and interview question. Do not mix these up. Git is the camera taking pictures of your code. GitHub is Instagram where you post the pictures for others to see. You can use Git without GitHub, but you cannot use GitHub without Git."

Slide 5: Core Vocabulary (Local)

Text:

Repository (Repo): The project folder that Git is actively tracking.

Commit: A saved "snapshot" of your code at a specific point in time, always accompanied by a message explaining why you made the change.

Speaker Notes: "When you type git commit, you are permanently saving the state of your files at that exact second. A project is just a long history of hundreds of commits. Your commit message should always be clear, like 'Added login button', not 'Fixed stuff'."

Slide 6: Core Vocabulary (Cloud)

Text:

Push: Uploading your local commits from your laptop up to the remote GitHub server.

Pull: Downloading the latest commits from the remote GitHub server down to your laptop.

Speaker Notes: "You will use these two commands every single day of your career. When you finish your work for the day, you push it to the cloud. When you start your work the next morning, you pull down whatever updates your teammates pushed while you were asleep."

Slide 7: The Standard Developer Workflow

Text:

Write Code -> 2. Save File -> 3. git commit (Snapshot) -> 4. git push (Upload).

Speaker Notes: "This is the heartbeat of DevOps. Once we learn this rhythm today, we will eventually connect step 4 to our CI/CD pipeline. Every time you 'push', the automated factory will wake up and start testing your code."

Slide 8: Lab Time!

Text: Today’s Goal:

Configure Git on your machine.

Create a local repository.

Make your first commit.

Push your code to the internet.

Speaker Notes: "Enough theory. Open up your terminals, log into GitHub, and let's get our hands dirty."

---

Here’s what was created for you.

### PowerPoint file
- **Path:** `d:\Teaching\SZABIST\SP26\TM_Labs\my-spa-project\Week5_Version_Control_Fundamentals.pptx`
- **Slides:** 8 (title + 7 content slides) matching your outline
- **Speaker notes:** Each slide has the notes you provided in the **Notes** pane (Presenter View will show them)

### Slide contents
1. **Title** — Week 5 title + Git & GitHub subtitle  
2. **“Final_v2_USE_THIS”** — three bullets (sharing code, concurrent edits, chaos)  
3. **Version control** — time machine + sandbox  
4. **Git vs. GitHub** — engine vs. cloud  
5. **Local vocabulary** — repo + commit  
6. **Cloud vocabulary** — push + pull  
7. **Workflow** — write → save → commit → push (with arrows)  
8. **Lab time** — “Today’s Goal” with indented sub-bullets  

### Regenerating later

Here’s what changed and where to find it.

### Visual design
- **Title slide:** Dark slate background (`#0f1728`), **white** bold title (40pt), **sky** italic subtitle (22pt), **cyan accent bar** along the bottom, and three small **“pipeline” dots** on the left for a light DevOps feel.
- **Content slides:** **Light gray** slide background (`#f8fafc`), **full-width navy ribbon** (`#1e3a5f`) for the title with **white** 28pt bold text, a **thin sky vertical bar** on the left edge, and **slate** body text (21pt) with slightly tighter line spacing.

### File updated
`Week5_Version_Control_Fundamentals.pptx` in your project root was **regenerated** with this styling (speaker notes are unchanged).

### Regenerating
```text
python scripts\build_week5_git_slides.py
```

Colors and layout are defined at the top of `scripts\build_week5_git_slides.py` as `C_*` constants, so you can tweak the palette there. In PowerPoint you can still apply a built-in **Design → Themes** variant on top if you want Microsoft’s slide master shapes as well.

_**User**_

I have generated this from Gemini. I want you to make changes as gemini stated. 
Since your students just finished their mid-terms, they’ll be in a "listener" mode. To deliver this effectively at SZABIST/MAJU, you should follow a "Problem → Solution → Implementation" flow.

Here is your step-by-step delivery guide for tomorrow’s session on useEffect and Data Persistence.

Step 1: The "Hook" (5 Minutes)
What to do: Open the "Quirky Fruity" project on the projector. Add 3-4 items to the cart so the MUI Badge shows a number.What to say: > "Class, look at our cart. It works great, right? But watch what happens when I refresh the page or if my laptop battery dies."
(Hit Refresh—the cart goes to 0).

"In a real e-commerce app, we just lost a customer. Today, we are going to give our app a Permanent Memory."

Step 2: The Concept – Browser Storage (10 Minutes)
Explain that the browser has a "Mini Database" called localStorage.

The Analogy: State is like a Whiteboard (erased when you leave the room). LocalStorage is like a Notebook (stays there even if you close the book).

The Rule: LocalStorage only saves Strings.

Step 3: The Tool – useEffect (15 Minutes)
Introduce the hook as a "Side Effect Manager."

How to explain it: "Normally, React code runs once when the component starts. useEffect allows us to run code whenever a specific variable changes."

The Logic: "We want to tell React: Every time the cartCount changes, automatically save it to the Notebook."

Step 4: Live Coding (20 Minutes)
Don't give them the code yet. Type it out on the screen in App.jsx and have them follow along.

Part A: Saving Data (The "Security Camera")

JavaScript
// 1. Import useEffect at the top
import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';

// 2. Inside your App component
useEffect(() => {
  // Key name is 'fruityCart', value is the state variable
  localStorage.setItem('fruityCart', cartCount);
}, [cartCount]); // Dependency array: only runs when cartCount changes
Part B: Loading Data (The "Hydration")
Now, show them how to retrieve that data when the app starts so the count isn't 0.

JavaScript
// Modify your initial state
const [cartCount, setCartCount] = useState(() => {
  const savedValue = localStorage.getItem('fruityCart');
  return savedValue ? parseInt(savedValue) : 0;
});
Step 5: The "Automation" Talk (10 Minutes)
As a technical professional, connect this to Automation.

Explain that we have Automated the Sync.

The user doesn't have to click "Save Cart." The useEffect hook handles the background process automatically. This is a core principle of Service Workers and Progressive Web Apps (PWAs).

Step 6: The Lab Task (30 Minutes)
Give them a challenge to prove they understood:

Task: Create a "Clear Cart" button in the Navbar.

Requirement: When clicked, it must set the cartCount to 0 AND clear the localStorage using localStorage.clear() or localStorage.removeItem('fruityCart').

---

_**Agent (composer-2-fast)**_

Here’s what was implemented to match Gemini’s **problem → solution → implementation** flow in code.

### `App.jsx` — persistence & “automation”
- **`useState` lazy init** reads `localStorage.getItem('fruityCart')`, uses `parseInt(..., 10)`, and falls back to `0` if missing or invalid (slightly safer than the snippet alone).
- **`useEffect` with `[cartCount]`** keeps the “notebook” in sync: non-zero values are written with `String(cartCount)` so it’s obvious you’re storing strings; when the cart is **0**, the effect **`removeItem`s** the key so storage stays clean after a clear (avoids immediately putting `'0'` back after Clear cart).
- **`FRUITY_CART_KEY`** constant set to `'fruityCart'` so the name matches the guide.
- **`clearCart`** sets count to `0` and calls `localStorage.removeItem('fruityCart')` as in the lab wording.

### `Navbar.jsx` + `Navbar.css` — lab task
- **“Clear cart”** MUI `Button` next to the badge, wired to `onClearCart`.
- Styling uses your existing accent (`#d5522f`) and Baloo 2; mobile tweaks keep the button readable.

### How this supports your session
1. **Hook** — Add to cart, refresh: count stays (after this change); **Clear cart** removes persistence again.
2. **Concept** — Comments in code can echo whiteboard vs notebook; `String(cartCount)` reinforces “strings only.”
3. **useEffect** — Side effect = sync to `localStorage` whenever `cartCount` changes.
4. **Live coding** — You can still re-type the same blocks in class; the repo now matches the target solution.
5. **Automation** — No “Save cart” click; sync runs from the effect (ties to PWAs/service workers verbally).

If you want a **starter repo without** the Clear cart button for students, say so and we can strip that while leaving persistence in place.

Here’s what changed.

### `App.jsx`
- **Import:** `import { useState } from 'react'; // 1. Import the State tool` is **commented**; the active line is `import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';`.
- **Constant:** Short note that there was **no** `FRUITY_CART_KEY` before; **new** `const FRUITY_CART_KEY = 'fruityCart';` stays active.
- **State:** `// const [cartCount, setCartCount] = useState(0);` then the **lazy** `useState(() => { ... })` for hydration.
- **Effect:** Note that there was **no** `useEffect` before, then the **live** persistence `useEffect`.
- **Clear cart:** Note that **`clearCart` didn’t exist**, then the real `clearCart` function.
- **JSX:** `{/* <Navbar count={cartCount} /> */}` plus `<Navbar count={cartCount} onClearCart={clearCart} />`.

### `Navbar.jsx`
- **Imports:** Comment that the **original** file only used Badge + icon; **`Button`** is still imported and used below.
- **Props:** `// const Navbar = ({ count = 0 }) => {` then **`const Navbar = ({ count = 0, onClearCart }) => {`**.
- **Cart block:** A **`{/* ... */}`** block holds the **old** markup (badge only); the **new** `<div className="nav-cart-wrap">` with **Badge + Clear cart** runs as normal.

Behavior is unchanged; `npm run build` passes. `Navbar.css` is unchanged (only layout for the new button). If you want the **pre-Gemini** CSS for `.nav-cart-wrap` commented above the new rules there too, say the word and we can mirror that pattern.

_**User**_

I am an instructor at SZABIST teaching Web Engineering. We are building a "Quirky Fruity" juice shop app. We have already implemented the UI, the Product Grid, the Cart State (Lifting State Up), and the Data Persistence.
Now, I need to implement Dynamic Routing. Please perform the following: (you are supposed to write comments on the lines which are being changed for understanding - use simple english)

Update App.jsx to include a dynamic route for /product/:id.

Create a new page file pages/ProductDetail.jsx that uses the useParams hook to identify the product and display its specific details (Description, Ingredients list, and Price).

Update the ProductCard.jsx component so that clicking the product image wraps it in a <Link> leading to that specific product's detail page.

Use the code snippets provided in the chat for the data structure and component logic."

Step 2: The Logic to Implement
1. Update App.jsx (Route Configuration)
This sets up the "placeholder" variable in your URL.

JavaScript
import ProductDetail from './pages/ProductDetail';
// ... other imports

function App() {
  // ... your existing persistence logic

  return (
    <Router>
      <Navbar count={cartCount} />
      <Routes>
        <Route path="/" element={<Home onAdd={addToCart} />} />
        {/* The ':id' is the dynamic part of our URL */}
        <Route path="/product/:id" element={<ProductDetail onAdd={addToCart} />} />
      </Routes>
    </Router>
  );
}
2. Create pages/ProductDetail.jsx (The Template)
This component acts as a "Search Party" to find and display the correct juice information.

JavaScript
import React from 'react';
import { useParams } from 'react-router-dom';
import { Container, Typography, Button, Box } from '@mui/material';

const ProductDetail = ({ onAdd }) => {
  const { id } = useParams(); // Grabs the ID from the URL

  // Data to filter through
  const products = [
    { id: 1, name: "Apple Juice", price: "$3.20", description: "Crisp and sweet Himalayan apples.", ingredients: ["Red Apples", "Honey", "Ice"] },
    { id: 2, name: "Orange Juice", price: "$3.20", description: "Zesty oranges from Sargodha.", ingredients: ["Oranges", "Mint"] },
    { id: 3, name: "Papaya Juice", price: "$3.20", description: "A tropical, vitamin-rich treat.", ingredients: ["Papaya", "Lime", "Natural Syrup"] },
  ];

  // Logic to find the matching product
  const product = products.find(p => p.id === parseInt(id));

  if (!product) return <Typography variant="h5">Product not found!</Typography>;

  return (
    <Container sx={{ py: 8 }}>
      <Box sx={{ display: 'flex', flexDirection: { xs: 'column', md: 'row' }, gap: 4 }}>
        <Box sx={{ flex: 1 }}>
          <Typography variant="h2" sx={{ fontWeight: 'bold', color: '#E15A44' }}>{product.name}</Typography>
          <Typography variant="h5" color="text.secondary" gutterBottom>{product.price}</Typography>
          <Typography variant="body1" sx={{ my: 3 }}>{product.description}</Typography>
          
          <Typography variant="h6">Ingredients:</Typography>
          <ul>
            {product.ingredients.map((ing, i) => <li key={i}>{ing}</li>)}
          </ul>

          <Button variant="contained" onClick={onAdd} sx={{ mt: 3, bgcolor: 'black', borderRadius: '50px' }}>
            Add to Cart
          </Button>
        </Box>
      </Box>
    </Container>
  );
};

export default ProductDetail;
3. Update components/ProductCard.jsx (The Trigger)
Wrap the image so it becomes clickable.

JavaScript
import { Link } from 'react-router-dom';

// Inside your ProductCard return:
<Link to={`/product/${id}`}>
  <img src={img} alt={name} />
</Link>

---
